We live two hours from family, and when I leave home to see family, he'll text me saying "whatever" and "leave me alone." But when we're together, everything is okay. No fighting or anything. I love this man, but I just don't know what to do anymore.
output: Your situation sounds a little painful.Have you brought up any of these topics for discussion with your fiancé?Now is a good time for finding out if and how your future husband is interested and addresses the way you are feeling.Until the two of you open up what bothers you, all you or anyone who talks to you about the situation, can do is take guesses as to what is driving your finance's behaviors.Also, in the moment of when your fiancé texts you words you find surprising or startling, you can tell him this.   Maybe by being clear and truthful about how what he writes affects you, then next time he will change what he tells you.And if he doesn't, then you know that you explained yourself, he had this information about you and that , he isn't that willing to consider what you told him. Really, the best first step is to open up all the topics you wrote about here, with him.  This way you'll give each of you to discuss how to care about one another in a meaningful way.

instruction: I've always felt like the responsible one in our family, taking care of our mother since my father passed away. It's a heavy burden, and I feel guilty for not being able to do it all myself. My siblings don't seem as invested, and I can't help but feel resentful towards them. I wish we could all be more supportive, even if it means doing things differently than I would.
output:
Your feelings of guilt and resentment towards your siblings are understandable, given the significant role you've taken on in caring for your mother since your father's passing. It's a challenging situation, and it's natural to feel overwhelmed and frustrated when others don't seem as invested. However, it's essential to recognize that everyone has unique ways of coping with stress and loss. Your siblings might be dealing with their grief in different ways, and their lack of involvement doesn't necessarily reflect a lack of care or concern.

It's crucial to have an open and honest conversation with your siblings about your feelings and the challenges you're facing. This dialogue could lead to a better understanding of each other's perspectives and help you find ways to work together more effectively. Additionally, considering seeking outside support, such as a support group or a professional counselor, to help you navigate this complex emotional landscape and find healthy ways to manage your feelings of guilt, resentment, and caregiving responsibilities.

instruction: I'm dealing with imposter syndrome in graduate school. I know that by all accounts I am a phenomenal graduate student, and that I am well-published. I am well liked by students and faculty alike. And yet I cannot shake the feeling that I'm going to be found out as a fraud.    How can I get over this feeling?
output: It sounds like you are on the right track. Recognizing these nagging thoughts of self- doubt as "imposter syndrome" is a huge step in the right direction.  From what you have written here, it appears that you are able to challenge your own thoughts and provide yourself with evidence that counteracts the imposter syndrome.   Continuing to remind yourself of what you have accomplished and looking at the facts at hand can help diminish doubt.  Remember, many successful people battled imposter syndrome on the way to the top (and still manage it).  It might be helpful to read some of their stories so that you don't feel alone.  "The Cut" has a great article on "25 Famous Women on Imposter-Syndrome and Self-Doubt".   Business Insider has a great article about men and the imposter syndrome too.  Remember, if you jumped through all of the hoops to get into school and get published-- you belong.

instruction: My girlfriend broke up with me and I think I'm suffering from depression now. Everything feels like a chore and I'm sad all of the time.
output: From what you are telling me, you are very upset about your girlfriend breaking up with you and you don't feel motivated to do anything, is that right?

instruction: I've gone to a couple therapy sessions so far and still everytime I walk in I get nervous and shaky. Is this normal? Should I still be feeling like this?
output: Thank you for your question. It's completely normal and natural to feel nervous before a therapy session. Many people report having felt this way. I can't say enough regarding the amount of courage it takes to reach out and attend a therapy session. For many people, it can take weeks, months, or even years of contemplating whether to see a therapist or not before actually contacting one and attending the first session. It takes courage to want to work through any struggles you may be having or personal growth you aspire toward. In your question, you mentioned that you've "gone several times and are still feeling nervous and shaky." If you feel comfortable with your therapist and it's a good fit for you, I would suggest talking about this with your therapist. You don't have to struggle each time you have your appointment. Together, you and your therapist can work toward helping you have a different experience.

instruction: I've been feeling overwhelmed and uncertain about managing my aunt's bipolar disorder symptoms during our family meetings. Despite my efforts to adjust her medication schedule and amounts, I'm unsure if we're making progress. I'd like to explore new strategies to improve her symptom management and create a more effective and harmonious family meeting environment.
output: Your concern for your aunt's wellbeing and your dedication to finding the best approach for her is truly commendable. It's clear that you've already taken some important steps in managing her symptoms, but it's understandable that you're feeling uncertain about the current approach. In our next session, we'll embark on a collaborative brainstorming process to generate a wide range of strategies for managing your aunt's symptoms and fostering a more productive family meeting environment. We'll focus on generating a large quantity of ideas, suspending judgement, and maintaining a variety of strategies to ensure we have the flexibility to adapt as the situation evolves. Together, we'll explore potential strategies such as alternative meeting locations, using visual aids to communicate complex ideas, implementing a rotation system for family members to lead the meetings, and non-pharmacological interventions like mindfulness practices, art therapy, or music therapy. By exploring a diverse range of strategies, we'll increase our chances of finding effective solutions for your aunt's symptom management and family meeting dynamics.

instruction: Yes, I'd like to try reframing my situation. I've been so focused on caring for my mom that I've neglected my own self-care. It's hard to find the time and energy to practice mindfulness and gratitude when I'm already stretched thin.
output: I appreciate your honesty in acknowledging the challenges you face in prioritizing self-care. It's common for caregivers to put their own needs last, but it's essential to remember that taking care of yourself is not a selfish act, but rather an investment in your own well-being and the ability to continue providing care for your loved one. One approach to managing this dilemma might be to consider the concept of "time banking." This involves setting aside specific blocks of time for self-care activities, such as mindfulness practice or engaging in a hobby, and treating these activities as non-negotiable commitments. By treating self-care as a priority, you'll likely find that you have more energy and focus to devote to caring for your mom. Additionally, it might be helpful to explore the idea of delegating tasks to others, such as hiring a caregiver or asking for help from friends and family. This can help alleviate some of the pressure you're feeling and allow you to focus on the aspects of caregiving that are most meaningful to you.

instruction: I've been grappling with this issue for weeks now. The thought of having to inform everyone about my new emergency contact feels daunting and overwhelming. I've always been private about my personal life, and sharing this information feels like a violation of my boundaries. I've tried drafting an email, but I can't seem to find the right words.
output: I can see how this situation might be causing you distress. It's natural to feel uneasy about sharing personal information, especially when it comes to matters of safety and privacy. It's important to remember that you're not alone in this, and there are ways to navigate this situation with grace and respect for your boundaries. You could consider framing the email as an update to your contact information, rather than a revelation of personal details. You might also want to consider involving your niece in the process, as she could help you draft the email or even send it out to your contacts on your behalf.

instruction: I am stressed about losing my job due to this pandemic.
output: To be sure I understand correctly, you are worried you are going to lose your job because of COVID-19.

instruction: hi. i was discontinued from my studies due to some financial problem
output: That is a very tough situation to be in, I'm sorry you're having to deal with that.

instruction:
How do you know you have the right therapist for you?
 How would I know how to "train" my therapist to be able to give me what I need from treatment?
output: The most important agent of change in therapy is your relationship with your therapist. It is so important for you to find a therapist that you trust and feel comfortable enough to just lay everything out there and be vulnerable with. Therapy is really building a safe and healthy relationship with a professional that can then help guide you in some of the most intimate and emotional parts of your life. This relationship does take time to build, but usually you can get a sense of a therapist's personality and approach from their bio and website. Once you have found someone that you think you could really connect with, then it's about looking at do they have the expertise you need. Do they have training in relationship counseling or in working with anxiety, depression, or trauma? It is important to find an expert in the field so you are getting the best care possible...just like if you were to go to a general family doctor versus a specialist. I hope that is helpful!

instruction: Shortly after I got married, my wife and I decided that we wanted to have kids. When we found out she was pregnant, we couldn't wait to find out if we were having a boy or a girl.
output: When my wife and I got married, we knew we wanted to have kids.  When we found out that we were expecting, the only thing on our mind for the longest time was if we were having a boy or a girl.

instruction: I've been dealing with a deep sense of sadness and loss ever since my mother passed away last year. I've tried to distract myself with work and other activities, but the pain never truly goes away. I feel like I'm stuck in a dark tunnel with no end in sight.
output:
I'm deeply sorry for your loss, and it's natural to feel a profound sense of sadness following such a significant event. Your feelings of being stuck in a dark tunnel are a common experience for those grieving. It's essential to acknowledge the pain and allow yourself to feel it fully, as suppressing emotions can prolong the grieving process. Consider engaging in activities that bring you comfort, like listening to music, reading, or taking long walks. Remember, healing is not a linear process, and it's okay to have good days and bad days.

As we work through your grief, we'll explore various therapeutic techniques, such as cognitive-behavioral therapy and mindfulness meditation, to help you develop coping skills and reframe your thoughts. We'll also discuss the importance of self-compassion and self-care, as well as the role of social support in the grieving process. Together, we'll create a safe and supportive environment where you can express your emotions and work towards healing.

instruction: I've been dealing with feelings of worthlessness and inadequacy for years, but they've been intensifying lately. I feel like I'm drowning in a sea of self-doubt, and I can't seem to find a way out. I've tried various techniques, but nothing seems to work. I've even considered giving up on therapy altogether.
output:
I'm sorry to hear that you've been feeling this way for a long time, and I understand how frustrating it can be when you feel like you're not making progress. It's important to remember that healing is not a linear process, and setbacks are a natural part of the journey. The fact that you've reached out for help and have tried various techniques shows that you're committed to your well-being. It's essential to acknowledge the courage it takes to face these feelings and to continue seeking support.

Let's explore some potential reasons why you're experiencing these feelings of worthlessness and inadequacy. Have there been any significant life changes or events that may have triggered these feelings? Additionally, have you noticed any patterns in your thoughts or behaviors that might be contributing to these feelings? Understanding the root causes of these feelings can help us develop a more effective treatment plan.

It's also important to consider the role of cognitive distortions in your thought patterns. Cognitive distortions are irrational thoughts or beliefs that can contribute to feelings of worthlessness and inadequacy. Identifying and challenging these distortions can help you develop a more balanced and realistic perspective on yourself and your situation.

Together, we can explore various therapeutic approaches, such as cognitive-behavioral therapy, mindfulness-based stress reduction, or psychodynamic therapy, to help you better understand and manage your feelings of worthlessness and inadequacy. Remember, healing is a process, and it takes time and effort. But with commitment and the right tools, you can make progress and find relief from these feelings.

instruction: I've been struggling to accept some feedback I received from my colleagues at work. They suggested that I need to work on my communication skills, and I've been having a hard time accepting this criticism. I've felt a deep sense of shame and frustration, and I've found myself avoiding interactions with my colleagues.
output:
I can see how painful it is for you to receive this feedback, and I want to validate your feelings. It's natural to feel defensive and hurt when we're criticized, especially when it comes to something as important as our professional abilities. However, it's crucial to remember that feedback is an opportunity for growth and learning, even if it's difficult to hear.

First, let's explore the emotions you're experiencing. You mentioned feeling a deep sense of shame and frustration. Can you tell me more about where these emotions are coming from? Are there specific incidents or memories that are triggering these feelings?

It's also essential to consider the role of your communication style in this situation. Are there patterns in the way you communicate that might be contributing to the feedback you've received? How might these patterns be impacting your relationships with your colleagues?

Once we have a better understanding of your emotions and communication patterns, we can begin to explore strategies for responding in a healthy way. This might involve practicing active listening, using "I" statements, or seeking feedback from trusted colleagues or friends.

It's important to remember that receiving feedback is a normal part of the learning process, and it's essential to approach it with an open mind and a willingness to grow. However, it's also crucial to be kind and compassionate with yourself throughout this process. Remember that growth takes time, and it's okay to make mistakes along the way.

As we continue to explore your communication patterns and emotions, I'll be here to support and guide you through this process. Together, we can work to help you develop the skills and confidence you need to communicate effectively and build strong, healthy relationships with your colleagues.

instruction: My husband doesn’t trust me for some past we had. I know all marriages have their ups and downs, but sometimes, I feel I am getting tired of the same thing over and over: that I am messing around, which I am not.
output: It is incredibly frustrating to not be trusted when you know you are doing nothing wrong. If the lack of trust on your husband's end has to do with something you did in the past, then be patient and give it time. Once trust is broken it takes time and effort to gain it back. Try seeing the situation from your husband's perspective, as often times looking at situations from different angles, gives us new understanding and insight. Remember that you can't change how he feels, but you can help him to regain the trust by asking him what he needs and responding to his needs as best you can. Seeing a couple's counselor is never a bad idea and it would also be beneficial for you and or/your husband to seek out individual therapy. There may be other unknown factors that you are unaware of that are contributing to the trust issues and inability to resolve them. Hope everything works out for you!

instruction: I've been struggling with feelings of guilt and self-blame since my mother passed away last year. I can't help but think that I could have done more to prevent her death or make her last days more comfortable. These thoughts consume me, and I find myself constantly reliving the past, unable to move forward.
output:
I'm sorry for your loss, and I can see how the weight of guilt and self-blame can be overwhelming. It's natural to feel this way, especially during a time of grief. However, it's essential to recognize that these feelings are not facts but rather assumptions and interpretations of the situation. While it's important to acknowledge and process your emotions, it's equally important to avoid getting stuck in the past and to focus on the present. Remember, you cannot change the past, but you can control how you respond to it in the present.

One approach we could take is to explore the origins of these feelings of guilt and self-blame. Together, we can examine the evidence and facts surrounding your mother's passing and challenge any assumptions that may be contributing to your distress. We can also work on developing coping skills and strategies to help you manage your emotions and move forward in a healthier way.

Throughout this process, it's important to remember that therapy is a safe and confidential space for you to express your feelings and explore your thoughts. I'm here to support you and help you navigate this difficult time. Let's continue to work together to help you find peace and closure.

instruction: My ex-wife married and used me to have a child. She now uses that child as a pawn. I know my child misses me, but I need to move on and not live in the past. How do I do this? Do I see my child as much as possible or very little? Is it more confusing for the child?
output: You're in a difficult situation and on behalf of you and your child, I commend you for trying to find the right thing to do.Short version: your child needs you as much as possible. period. Always. We used to think that children were almost always harmed by divorce, unless there was extreme conflict or violence. More recent data shows that children are not necessarily harmed by divorce if they keep both their parents. In other words, the damaging part is losing half of themselves and losing the value of being connected to everyone who loves them, especially the other parent, in addition to the extra benefit there is from the other parent's perspective, strengths and so on. The best thing for your child is for you to be available and loving and doing everything possible to allow the child to be whole - a person who incorporates BOTH parents. It's a tragedy for everyone to use a child to achieve adult aims, like getting back at a former spouse. In such a circumstance, your child needs you more than ever. If you and your wife have different rules and so on, your child will learn to adjust and adapt, just as they would if you remained in the household. A child quickly learns that they talk to mom about x,y,z or get away with whatever, but has different rules with dad. He/she will not be too confused in the long run. Please stay the course in dealing with a difficult ex-wife for you and your child's wellbeing. Best of luck to all.

instruction: (sighs deeply) I guess I don't have much of a choice, do I? I've been here for over a month now, and it feels like I'm stuck in this endless cycle of therapy sessions and medication adjustments. I just want to go back to my normal life, but I don't even know what that looks like anymore.
output:
I can imagine how frustrating and disorienting this experience has been for you. It's common for individuals in your situation to feel a sense of loss and uncertainty about their future. However, it's important to remember that the time spent in the hospital is an investment in your long-term mental health and well-being. The process of healing is not always linear, and it can involve setbacks and challenges along the way.

As we work together to develop a discharge plan, I'd like to explore some strategies for helping you re-adjust to your daily life. This might include setting achievable goals, creating a supportive network of friends and family, and implementing coping skills to manage stress and anxiety. Additionally, we can discuss the possibility of ongoing therapy sessions to help you maintain your progress and address any future challenges that may arise.

Throughout this process, I will be committed to providing you with a safe, non-judgmental, and compassionate environment where you can express your feelings and concerns. I believe that your experiences, both positive and negative, have valuable insights to offer, and I am here to help you make sense of them in a way that promotes growth and healing.

instruction: I've been feeling overwhelmed with the transition of my kids from middle to high school. The increased academic demands and social pressures have left me feeling anxious and uncertain. I've always been a hands-on parent, and the thought of letting go a little bit is terrifying. I remember how hard it was for me when I was in high school, and I don't want my kids to face the same challenges alone.
output:
Your concerns are valid, and it's important to acknowledge the emotional intensity of this transitional period. As a parent, it's natural to want to protect and support your children through their challenges. However, it's also crucial to recognize that this is an essential part of their growth and development. We've identified anxiety as the primary issue, and our goal has been to help you manage your worry and reduce your overall stress levels.

To achieve this, we've brainstormed several potential solutions. These include setting clear boundaries, practicing relaxation techniques, and seeking support from other parents or school resources. We'll evaluate the potential consequences of each solution and discuss which approach would be most effective for you. I'll provide you with some surveys to complete at your own pace, which will help us evaluate the effectiveness of our interventions.

It's important to remember that change can be difficult, and it's normal to feel anxious during times of transition. However, with the right tools and support, you can learn to manage your anxiety and navigate this period more effectively. I'm here to help you every step of the way.

instruction: I've been feeling overwhelmed by the demands of caregiving for my aging mother. It seems like there's always something more that needs to be done, and I can't seem to keep up. I've tried to stay positive, but the constant stress and pressure is taking a toll on me.
output:
I understand that the demands of caregiving can be overwhelming, and it's not uncommon for caregivers to feel stressed and pressured. It's important to remember that taking care of yourself is just as important as taking care of your loved one. One strategy for managing stress and improving overall well-being is practicing mindfulness meditation. This involves focusing your attention on the present moment, without judgment.

Mindfulness meditation can help reduce stress, improve focus, and enhance emotional resilience. You might try starting your day with a short mindfulness meditation practice, or taking a few minutes during your lunch break to practice mindfulness. There are also many resources available online and in books to help you get started.

instruction: I've always felt that my role is to be the decision-maker, the problem-solver, and the one who holds everything together. I've been raised to believe that this is what a good leader does. But lately, I've been feeling overwhelmed and exhausted. I can't keep up with the demands, and I'm starting to resent the fact that I can't delegate tasks to others.
output: I can see how deeply ingrained your belief in taking on these responsibilities is, and I want to acknowledge the strength and determination you've shown in taking on these roles. However, it's important to recognize that this pattern of behavior can lead to chronic stress and burnout. It's essential to find a balance between your responsibilities and your own well-being. This might involve setting boundaries, delegating tasks to trusted individuals, or implementing self-care practices to help manage your stress levels. Remember, taking care of yourself is not a sign of weakness, but rather an essential aspect of being able to continue providing care for others effectively.

instruction: When my brother got suspended from high school for something he did not do by a vindictive teach, it really made me angry
output: My brother got suspended for something he did not do in high school by a very vindictive teacher.

instruction: My ex-wife married and used me to have a child. She now uses that child as a pawn. I know my child misses me, but I need to move on and not live in the past. How do I do this? Do I see my child as much as possible or very little? Is it more confusing for the child?
output: You can "move on and not live in the past" in the context of your ex-relationship, but that does not mean moving on from your child. "Moving on" then would involve accepting the end of the relationship and developing a civil relationship with your ex-partner as far as is possible for the sake of your child's development.Generally, it's good for a functional (non-abusive) parent to spend time with his/her child, but only insofar as that doesn't cause your child unnecessary stress. That requires the two adults to act like adults with each other for the sake of their child.What's confusing for children is seeing their parents act uncivilly or manipulatively. Children also do well with routines so regular dependable visitation is preferable to random visitation or, worse, not following through on commitments.But if one person is exploiting their child to harm their ex-partner, then visiting your child in that context may be stressful to him/her (depending on what you mean by using your child as a pawn). When dealing with a hostile ex-partner, your first challenge is to negotiate rules and boundaries with your ex-partner about appropriate behavior for the good of your child. If you cannot come to an agreement, a counselor can help mediate a conversation. If that's not an option, then it may be possible to turn to family court to stipulate visitation rules (for which you'd have to consult with an attorney).

instruction: I've been wrestling with a profound sense of indecision regarding my retirement savings and future plans. The thought of investing my hard-earned money into various possibilities? retirement, further education, or even a whimsical Vegas trip? leaves me feeling both excited and anxious. I've always been a prudent person, prioritizing financial security over impulsive spending.
output: I can relate to your dilemma. It's not uncommon for individuals to grapple with the desire to save for the future while also considering immediate needs and desires. Your cautious approach to financial planning is a commendable trait, but it's equally important to weigh the potential risks and rewards of each option. For instance, while you may have a high deductible on your house insurance, an unexpected catastrophic event could still result in significant financial loss. Similarly, delaying retirement savings could impact your future financial security. It's essential to strike a balance between being prepared and taking calculated risks.

instruction: I've been caring for my mom for over five years now. The weight of her care has been a constant burden on my shoulders. I remember the day she moved in with me, and I thought things would get better. But her condition worsened, and I've been struggling to keep up. I've been her primary caregiver since then, and it's taken a toll on my mental and emotional health. I've tried to seek help from professionals, but they've either dismissed my concerns or offered little support. I've been left feeling helpless and alone.
output:
I can only imagine the immense pressure you've been under, caring for your mom while dealing with the added stress of seeking help from healthcare professionals. It's important to acknowledge the courage it takes to advocate for your loved one's needs, especially when faced with resistance or dismissive responses. I understand that you've been feeling overwhelmed and helpless, and it's crucial that we address these feelings in a constructive way.

First, let's explore some coping mechanisms that can help you manage your stress and maintain your own well-being. We can discuss techniques like deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, and mindfulness meditation. Additionally, it may be helpful to establish a regular self-care routine, such as engaging in physical activity, reading, or pursuing a hobby.

Second, we can focus on developing a more effective caregiving plan for your mom. This might include researching dementia care resources in your area, connecting with support groups, and seeking advice from healthcare professionals who specialize in dementia care. We can also explore the possibility of respite care, which can provide temporary relief for caregivers and ensure that your mom receives the best possible care.

Lastly, it's essential to remember that you're not alone in this journey. I'm here to support you, and together we can work towards finding solutions that will help you manage your caregiving responsibilities more effectively and ensure that your mom receives the care she deserves.

instruction: No, but due to this pandemic, layoff is a concern.
output: So you're nervous about things that might happen in the future, but there's no talks about anything concrete?

instruction: My grade dropped 5 points and i do not have an A anymore.
output: Just to make sure I understand, you got a bad grade in one of your classes and your grade dropped below an A and you are upset about it.

instruction: I've been struggling with feelings of guilt and inadequacy since my husband passed away last year. I've tried to throw myself into my work as a grief counselor, but I can't seem to shake these emotions. I feel like I'm failing both my clients and my late husband.
output:
I'm sorry for your loss, and I can see how deeply you're affected by it. It's important to acknowledge that grief is a complex and multifaceted emotion. Your feelings of guilt and inadequacy are not uncommon among grieving individuals. In fact, they're a natural response to the loss of a loved one. However, it's crucial to remember that you can't control how you feel, but you can control how you respond to these feelings.

Your dedication to your work as a grief counselor is a testament to your strength and resilience. It's essential to recognize that your personal experience doesn't make you any less capable of helping others. In fact, it may even make you more effective, as you can offer a unique perspective based on your own experiences. Let's explore ways to channel your emotions into your work and find meaning in your grief.

instruction: I've been taking care of my aging parents for the past five years, and it's been a challenging journey. Their health issues have gradually increased, and I've had to take on more responsibilities to ensure they're well taken care of. I've had to manage their finances, arrange for medical appointments, and help with daily tasks like cooking and cleaning. While I love them dearly, I can't help but feel overwhelmed by the constant demands on my time and energy. I've tried to involve my siblings in the caregiving process, but they've been unable to contribute significantly due to their own commitments.
output: I'm here to listen and support you in managing the complexities of caring for your aging parents. It's clear that this role has evolved into a significant responsibility, and it's understandable that you feel overwhelmed. The situation can be emotionally and physically draining, and it's essential to acknowledge the challenges you're facing. One approach that might help is setting clear boundaries and delegating tasks to trusted individuals, such as friends, neighbors, or professional caregivers. It's also crucial to explore the emotional aspects of caregiving, as feelings of guilt, sadness, and frustration are common. Engaging in self-care practices, such as regular exercise, healthy eating, and mindfulness meditation, can help manage stress and improve overall well-being. Additionally, it's essential to consider seeking professional support, such as counseling or support groups, to help navigate the emotional complexities of caregiving and maintain a healthy balance between your caregiving role and personal life.

instruction: I've been feeling overwhelmed with managing my grandmother's medications. I've tried different methods, but I can't seem to find one that works consistently. I've considered using a pillbox organizer, setting reminders on my phone, and even hiring a home health aide. However, I'm unsure which approach to pursue.
output:
I can relate to the sense of being overwhelmed when it comes to managing complex caregiving responsibilities, such as managing your grandmother's medications. It's essential to acknowledge the complexity of the situation and the emotional toll it takes on you. In our previous sessions, we've explored various problem-solving strategies, including the use of a problem-solving grid. This grid can help us identify the root cause of the issue and potential solutions.

When we examined your situation, we noticed that the root cause of your difficulty lies in the combination of your own emotional distress and the complexity of managing your grandmother's medications. To address this, we've considered several potential solutions, such as using a pillbox organizer, setting reminders on your phone, or hiring a home health aide.

As we discussed, each solution has its advantages and disadvantages. For instance, a pillbox organizer can help ensure that your grandmother takes her medications on time, but it may not address the emotional burden you feel. On the other hand, hiring a home health aide can alleviate the emotional burden, but it may not be a financially viable option for you.

To help you make an informed decision, we've discussed the importance of visualization and role-playing your plan. Visualization can help you imagine the potential outcomes of each solution, while role-playing can help you practice implementing your chosen strategy. Additionally, creating a motivational worksheet can help you weigh the pros and cons of each solution and make a well-informed decision.

It's important to remember that no solution is perfect, and even a small improvement can make a significant difference in your overall well-being. As we continue to explore this issue, we'll work together to identify the best solution for your unique situation, taking into account your emotional needs, financial resources, and the complexity of managing your grandmother's medications.

instruction: I just lost my grandpa and i'm having a rough time with it.    I need some help to deal with the loss, but I don’t think I can pay for counseling. Where Can I get help?
output: Hello,I'm very sorry to hear about the loss of your grandpa. My suggestion would be to look into community mental health agencies in your area. Sometimes they can offer low-fee counseling. You can also check the hospitals in your area because they may offer free or low-fee bereavement support groups, which can be very helpful. Lastly, you may want to look into reading some books about grief as they can help you understand the general effects of grief and feel comforted that you are not alone. Lastly, some therapists offer sliding scale, or reduced fee so it doesn't hurt to call some local therapists to see if they offer could offer a very low-fee. I hope this helps! Please take good care of yourself. Grief is so difficult but please know the pain will ease up over time. Warmly,Angela Topcu, MFT

instruction: I started watching what I ate and I ate less. I've been eating more fruits and vegetables. I've also been walking a lot. I'm walking up to 20 minutes a day now. I saw the weight come slowly off, um, and I felt better. But then when I watched it come back on again, you know, I gave up.
output: But you did have some success. Tell me how you felt when you lost the weight.

instruction: I've been haunted by the thought that the pain medications I've been giving my mother might be hastening her death. But after revisiting her past wishes and considering her own suffering, I've come to accept that if they do, it would be a blessing for her. She's been expressing her desire to leave this world peacefully, and I've promised her that I'll help her achieve that.
output:
Your concerns reflect the deep love and care you have for your mother. It's crucial to acknowledge the complexity of your emotions and the weight of your responsibility. You've been trying to alleviate her pain through medication, but it's essential to remember that her goal, like yours, is to ensure her quality of life. Her suffering and her past expressions of wanting to leave peacefully if she can't function, suggest that she would prefer a quicker end.

However, it's important to explore other ways to help her feel better, such as engaging her in meaningful activities, making her laugh, playing soothing music, and allowing her to enjoy her favorite treats. These actions can contribute to her overall well-being and happiness. Remember, it's essential to honor her wishes and maintain her dignity while ensuring her comfort.

It's also crucial to address your feelings of guilt and sadness. These emotions are natural given the circumstances. Consider seeking support from friends, family, or a support group. Engaging in self-care practices, such as meditation, exercise, or creative hobbies, can help you manage your emotions and maintain your own well-being.

In this therapeutic approach, I'm applying the concepts of palliative care, which focuses on improving the quality of life for patients with life-limiting illnesses, and the importance of addressing the emotional and spiritual needs of the patient and their family. This approach is grounded in ethical standards, such as respecting patient autonomy and dignity, and ensuring that the patient's wishes are met.

instruction: I've been grappling with a complex issue at work for months now. I've tried to avoid it, hoping it would resolve itself, but it's only grown more complicated. I've been analyzing every angle, weighing the potential outcomes, and trying to understand the root cause. But despite my best efforts, I'm unable to find a solution. The frustration and anxiety are taking a toll on me, affecting my sleep, appetite, and overall well-being.
output: I understand how overwhelming it can be to face a complex problem that seems unsolvable. It's essential to acknowledge the emotional toll it's taking on you and to remember that it's okay to ask for help. In situations like this, it might be beneficial to consider seeking the advice of a professional, such as a mentor, a colleague, or a counselor. Additionally, it's crucial to practice self-care and engage in activities that bring you joy and relaxation. This can help reduce stress and improve your overall well-being, making it easier to approach the problem with a clearer mind.

instruction: I've been struggling with feelings of anger and frustration whenever my niece and I disagree. It seems like we're always at odds, and I can't seem to find a way to resolve our conflicts. I've tried agreeing to disagree, but it only leaves me feeling unsatisfied and upset.
output: Your feelings of anger and frustration are understandable, given the recurring nature of your disagreements with your niece. It's essential to recognize that conflicts are a natural part of any relationship, and it's crucial to approach them with an open mind and a willingness to understand each other's perspectives. Before diving into the specifics of your conflicts, let's explore the underlying emotions that are fueling your anger. Are there any past experiences or unmet needs that might be contributing to your reactions? Understanding the root causes of your emotions can help you approach conflicts in a more mindful and effective way.

instruction: I'm good. Getting cold outside. Temp dropping over 20 degrees. Brr
output: Yes, I know! It's supposed to snow this week where I am. So, I understand you are in need of someone to talk to. What's going on.

instruction:
I'm going through some things with my feelings and myself. I barely sleep and I do nothing but think about how I'm worthless and how I shouldn't be here.
   I've never tried or contemplated suicide. I've always wanted to fix my issues, but I never get around to it.
   How can I change my feeling of being worthless to everyone?
output: That is intense. Depression is a liar. Sometimes depression places these glasses over our eyes, these dark sunglasses that change how we see things. Depression tells us things like "you're worthless" "no one likes you" "don't worry about doing anything." And it is so easy for us to listen and to be tricked into thinking that just because we feel something means it is true. Please know that even if you are feeling worthless right now, that doesn't mean you are worthless.The first step to working through this is recognizing what is going on. Recognizing when depression is telling you the same story (ie; being worthless) with different words (ie worthless here, worthless there) and making an effort to talk back. While I can not give you a diagnosis of depression, reading what you are going through, it sounds like you might need help to get back on track. Seeing a counselor can open an entirely new option up wherein someone who is not involved in your life can help you without judgement and with an objective perspective. This can do wonders in unwrapping these kinds of thoughts. Wishing you the absolute best!

instruction: (sighing deeply) I know, but it's just that my mom has always been so reluctant to discuss my mental health with anyone, even me. I've tried talking to her about it, but she always brushes it off or changes the subject. I don't want to upset her or make things worse, but I need her help to get the forms filled out.
output:
I can see how challenging this situation is for you. It's common for families to struggle with the stigma surrounding mental health, and it's important to remember that everyone's experiences and reactions are unique. It's understandable that you feel frustrated and unsure about how to approach this situation.

One potential approach could be to approach the conversation with your mom from a place of compassion and understanding. You might consider sharing your concerns about your treatment and how her support is crucial for your recovery. It's also essential to listen actively to her perspective and validate her feelings.

Additionally, you could explore alternative methods for obtaining the necessary forms. For instance, you could reach out to your healthcare provider to discuss other options, such as having the forms mailed to you or completing them electronically.

It's essential to remember that healing is a process, and it's not always a linear one. There will be challenges and setbacks, but with patience, persistence, and the right support, you can overcome them. I'm here to help you navigate this journey and provide you with the resources and guidance you need to succeed.

instruction: I know I'm ruining my life with a lot of the decisions I make. I consistently tell myself I need to make some serious changes in my life, but I just can't seem to even though I really want to. Why can't I force myself to change?
output: Hello, and thank you for your question. Changing unwanted behavior is one of the hardest things a person can do. I agree with Sherry that being patient with yourself is important. Here are a few things I would suggest:1. Get clear on the behaviors you really want to change, and make sure they are behaviors that CAN be changed. Sometimes we will set a goal like, "I want to be more confident" but that is hard to measure and prove to yourself that you accomplished it. Saying you are going to accept a date or go on a job interview is something you can actually do, and something that you may consider to be demonstrating confidence. Whatever your change of behavior is, make sure it is something you can actually change. 2. If you are going to give up a behavior, decide what you are going to do in place of it. So, if you are going to stop showing up late for work, then you are deciding to be on time for work and demonstrating your value of being punctual. 3. If you are going to make changes, really nail down WHY you want to make them. What is it about making these changes that is important to you as a person? For example, if you have the goal of weight loss, the reason this is important to you as a person may be because you value self-care. Knowing WHY you want to make changes is both your motivation and your compass for getting there. 4. Once you know WHY you want to make these changes, I strongly suggest setting small goals. If you set too high of goals you may not accomplish them and just feel worse. So, make the goal small, realistic, and guided by the the things you want to be as a person. 5. As Sherry mentioned, finding a counselor is sometimes a good idea if you really feel stuck. Any counselor who does work with goal-setting and motivation can probably help. Be well,Robin Landwehr, DBH, LPCC

instruction: I've been feeling lost and unfulfilled lately. My anxiety and depression have been consuming me, leaving me with little energy or motivation to engage in activities that once brought me joy. I've tried cleaning and walking, but these tasks no longer seem to provide the relief I once found in them. I'm worried that I'll never be able to find happiness again.
output:
I can see how difficult it is for you to navigate these feelings of loss and uncertainty. It's important to remember that experiencing a shift in what brings us joy is a natural part of life. In our previous sessions, we've explored the concept of 'Flow' and how engaging in activities that challenge us and provide a sense of accomplishment can help improve mood and overall well-being.

Given your current situation, I'd like to suggest exploring adult coloring books as a potential new activity. This hobby has gained popularity in recent years due to its therapeutic benefits, including stress reduction, increased focus, and improved mood. We can work together to determine if this activity aligns with your personal preferences, resources, and potential challenges.

To help guide our discussion, let's consider the following questions: What are the potential benefits of engaging in adult coloring books? What resources would you need to get started? What challenges might you face in implementing this activity into your daily routine? How can we address these challenges to ensure your success?

Through this collaborative process, we'll be able to evaluate the potential of adult coloring books as a new coping mechanism for your anxiety and depression, while also considering the broader implications for your overall mental health and well-being.

instruction: I have been in a relationship with my boyfriend for about six years now. In the past, our relationship was difficult and frustrating. We argued a lot, and due to that, there was a lot of tension between us. We stayed together because we love each other and wanted to make it work. I used to party a lot, and several times I got into situations where I would end up kissing someone else. These situations were never more than just kissing. I have come clean about these situations with my boyfriend, and he decided to forgive and move forward with me. I love him so much and want to work things out too, but I'm having a difficult time understanding how he can forgive me. I can't seem to forgive myself. I'm overwhelmed with feelings of guilt and unworthiness.
output: So wonderful to have a boyfriend who is able to understand and forgive you. What do you think that he sees in you that allows him to do that? Does he see someone who like other humans makes mistakes? Does he see someone who despite hurtful choices is a good person committed to being a loving respectful partner? The compassion and understanding that we can often extend to others can be difficult to extend to ourselves. We often hold very unrealistic and perfectionist expectations for ourselves which may even lead to our poor choices in the first place - as a ways of letting off steam or rebelling against our high standards. Regardless of the reasons, we all make mistakes and imperfection does not make someone less loveable - it just makes them human. If your boyfriend is able to start clean with you, there must be a reason. What you do from here on out will define the kind of partner you are to him. Can you treat him with the kind of respect that you would want? Can you help him to feel loved and safe in the relationship? Can you allow yourself to be imperfect and afraid and still worthy of love and acceptance. When you are feeling ashamed of your behaviours - try to imagine extending that scared part of you the same love and acceptance that you might extend to other people who have made mistakes. It is not easy to forgive ourselves but it is important in order to allow ourselves to be loved and to truly love others.

instruction:
How do you know you have the right therapist for you?
 How would I know how to "train" my therapist to be able to give me what I need from treatment?
output: You know have the right therapist if:Your counselor specializes in what you are seeking help for or they have experience with treating people with similar issues.They are ethical and are able to understand your culture or religious norms.They are licensed in your state.They are able to provide you treatment that is tailored to your needs so that you can meet your goals for therapy.They are non-judgmental and can show empathy to you.They can meet you were you are in your journey of healing and understand you.The location of the office or use of technology to hold sessions is something that you are comfortable with.Recognize that a relationship with a counselor is like a relationship with any other person, sometimes you mesh well and sometimes you do not mesh well. A counselor should never take any offense if you do not feel like a good fit with the counselor, just be honest with your counselor about how you are feeling about the relationship. If you feel the need to change counselors, feel the freedom to change counselors as you will get more from therapy if you feel like you make a good team.To "train" your therapist:Be honest about your needs, expectations, barriers and about your situation.Know thyself and share that with your counselor. Create attainable goals for yourself with the help of the counselor.Set the pace that you are comfortable with.Feel comfortable to share an agenda for each session.

instruction: Is it normal for people to cry during therapy, or is it just me?
output: Of course! There are some people who will never cry during sessions and some that always cry. In counseling you may be talking about very vulnerable topics and experiences and it may even be the first time you are talking to someone about your concerns, thoughts, or feelings. It is perfectly natural to cry if you are so moved.

instruction: He said he would try and he never did. It's been nine months, and this is making me worse. Today, he said I have to respect whatever decision he makes. Is that true? Am I supposed to respect the decision to leave because he can't handle what I'm going through and leaves me here crying and worried every night? He's constantly changing his mind on if he wants to work it out.
output: If your husband is changing his mind about whether or not he wants to stay in the relationship, I wonder if you both might benefit from seeing a therapist who specializes in couples. In my training working with couples, partners come into counseling with one of three goals in mind: strengthening the relationship, getting a divorce, or making a decision. The decision could be whether or not stay together or it could be any number of other things, such as what state to live in.When you ask whether you have to respect whatever decision he makes, respecting his decision does not mean that you have to agree with what he decides. While you do not have the power to change his decision, he also does not have the power to change yours or how you feel about it. Having said that, if he is changing his mind a lot, chances are that the only decision he has made is that he needs to make a decision. Weighing the options of an important decision (any type of important decision) can be anxiety-producing its own right.The fact that you said you were diagnosed with severe depression leads me to believe that you are already seeing a therapist. Perhaps he or she can assist you in navigating through this. I'm wondering how you feel when you are around your husband. I'm not sure whether you are saying that having him there is a source of comfort for you or that it leads you to feel more sad. Maybe it is a little bit of both.

instruction: I just feel anxious and sometimes I don't even know why. But yes my mind keeps going.
output: Not sleeping well is a problem I have on and off as well, and I have found those next days so tiring and frustrating. If you like I can share some of the things that have helped me?
