Almost every single person I have worked with has cried at one point or another.  Starting therapy takes a lot of courage; when you work with a therapist ,once you feel safe ,  you talk about a lot of very private and potentially emotional things that you may have never discussed with anyone before.  I have seen  people cry with sadness , cry with relief at releasing painful things they have held onto for a long time, cry because they feel so relieved that they finally feel heard.   I myself have sometimes cried with clients because some things they talk about are so profoundly moving.   I can assure you that most , if not all , therapists expect clients to cry  and there is nothing to be embarrassed about.   Crying can be very cathartic and can allow us to release a lot of painful feelings we have been stuffing down.   Crying is  often a sign that you are really working things through and getting in touch with feelings that you need to get in touch with in order to heal .So , to summarize , don't worry at all about crying! It will probably feel uncomfortable  at first , but I promise you , you will not be judged for crying in therapy.   And it will be less embarrassing once it happens a time or two and you are assured that your therapist will be there for you and won't judge you !Good luck !!

instruction:
I have secrets in my mind, and I don't know what to do with them. I don't want to tell my wife and mom because I don't want to hurt them. But I'm not sure how long that I can keep the secret to myself.

What should I do? It's becoming annoying and making me anxious. Help me out
output: It sounds like keeping the secrets has become a problem for you now. There are several things to consider before you make a decision.- You mentioned that you don't want your wife and mom to know because you don't want to hurt them – why would it hurt them? - Is it necessary for them to know this information?- What are the consequences of either telling them the truth or not telling them? (for you and for your wife and mom).- Once you have considered these, think of what you would tell your friend if they were in your exact situation?- Also, if your wife or mom were in your situation right now, what do you think they would do themselves?- If your wife and mom were in this situation, how would you feel? Would you want to know the secrets?- How has keeping these secrets affected your own mental and physical health?Once you have looked at the problem from all angles, you will be able to better make a decision on whether it is right to tell them or not.

instruction: When a friend did not come to my moms funeral and lied to me about why, it just really disappointed me. Its something that still makes me upset.
output: A friend lied to me about why he did not come to my moms funeral. It was stupid at the time but I am still upset about it.

instruction: I've been getting told lately by my partner that it’s embarrassing that I'm so antisocial around his family and friends. I believe it's because I'm not as social as him.
output: Hi Buffalo, I think you're right; your partner's reactions are about him. I have to say it's a cruel thing to say to someone you love...that you're "embarrassed by them". The subtext says "You're not good enough", and I love that you can reject that false message.You don't have to be different for anyone. If he can't accept you for who you are, what is he doing with you? Your social habits (unless they are extreme...like you refuse to go anywhere) aren't a reflection on him; it's just you being you. Also, and I'm sure you've found this...the more he judges you, the more difficult it is for you to be the confident, outgoing person he wants you to be...so his method of trying to change you doesn't even work!I think that's the crux...he's trying to change you. What does that make you want to do?

instruction: When my brother got a degree in college, I was proud of him. He was the first in our family to do that.
output: I was proud when my brother got his degree. He was the first in our family to do that so it was nice.

instruction: I've been struggling to balance my role as a caretaker in hospice with my responsibilities as a teacher. The demands of both roles have been overwhelming, and I've found myself feeling increasingly anxious and drained. I've been having trouble sleeping, and I've noticed that I've been snapping at my students more frequently than usual. I've tried to manage my time effectively, but it seems like there's never enough hours in the day.
output:
I can see how challenging it must be for you to juggle these two roles, especially given the emotional demands of caring for someone in hospice. It's important to acknowledge that you're carrying a heavy load right now, and it's understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed. I'd like to suggest a few strategies that might help you manage your time and reduce your stress levels.

First, it might be helpful to prioritize your tasks and delegate some responsibilities, if possible. Could you discuss your situation with your employer and see if they could provide you with some flexibility or support? Additionally, you might consider reaching out to the hospice agency to discuss any potential accommodations they could offer to help ease your workload.

Another strategy that might be helpful is practicing mindfulness meditation or deep breathing exercises to help manage your anxiety and improve your sleep quality. It's also important to ensure that you're taking care of yourself physically, by eating well, getting regular exercise, and staying hydrated.

Lastly, I'd like to offer you some additional resources that might be helpful in managing your stress and anxiety. I'll send you a list of local support groups and counseling services that specialize in caregiving and stress management. I'll also provide you with some self-help materials, such as books and online resources, that you can access at your own pace.

Remember, it's important to prioritize your own well-being during this time. I'll be here to support you throughout this process, and we'll follow up in about six weeks to review your progress and discuss any additional concerns or challenges you may be facing. In the meantime, please don't hesitate to reach out if you need anything.

instruction: I've been having trouble sleeping due to my husband's nightmares. He's been prescribed Haldol to minimize them, which has allowed me to get a good night's sleep in the same room with him. I manage to get around six or seven hours of sleep, but I wake up a few times during the night to use the bathroom.
output:
Your husband's nightmares have created a challenging situation for both of you. The Haldol medication has been a crucial step in managing the nightmares and enabling you to get a decent amount of sleep. However, I understand that your sleep quality could be improved. It's essential to maintain this routine, as it allows you to support your husband during the day and manage your daily responsibilities effectively.

Your sleep pattern, which includes six to seven hours of sleep with occasional bathroom breaks, is a common adaptation to disrupted sleep. However, I would like to suggest some additional strategies to help you improve the quality of your sleep. First, consider implementing a consistent bedtime routine, which may include activities like reading, taking a warm bath, or practicing relaxation techniques such as deep breathing or progressive muscle relaxation. These practices can help prepare your body for sleep and reduce the impact of any interruptions.

Second, you might find it helpful to create a sleep-conducive environment. This could involve using blackout curtains, keeping the bedroom cool, and minimizing external noise. Additionally, you could consider using a white noise machine or earplugs to help mask any disruptive sounds.

Lastly, I would encourage you to discuss this issue with your husband's doctor. It's essential to ensure that the Haldol dosage is optimal for both his nightmares and your sleep needs. This conversation could lead to further adjustments or alternative treatments that may benefit both of you.

instruction: I appreciate you saying that. It makes me feel a bit better! Do you have any advice about how I can take my mind off of things?
output: Do not let this affect your self esteem, as you are a valuable person. Take long walks, spend time enjoying your own company and know that you are worth it

instruction: I can understand that. But my job is really important right now as my partner lost his in COVID. and he is not doing anything right now not even helping at home. It makes me more low and it is hard to take care of 3 younger kids who are staying at home full time.
output: Ok, I understand better now. Thank you for explaining. What you are telling should to be something you need to discuss with your partner. Do both of you communicate well with each other? Maybe he is not aware of your situation or how you feel.

instruction: I am a survivor of domestic violence from a past relationship. Even after seven years, I still have horrible nightmares. I wake up in sweat, and the dreams feel so real.
output: Nightmare are actually very common for survivors of domestic violence. If they are affecting you the the point where you feel like you need to do something, you may want to consider processing your memories of the events with a trained trauma therapist. Another good option, may be to attend a support group for survivors where you could process your memories in a safe space where others will be able to provide support and understanding for what you went through as well as encouragement for the strength it took you to get out of that relationship. You may also want to consider completing a screening or assessment to rule out post-traumatic stress disorder as well.

instruction: I've been having intrusive thoughts about harming myself, and it's been getting worse over the past few weeks. I've tried to reach out to my support system, but I feel like I'm a burden. I don't want to be a danger to myself or others, but I don't know what to do.
output: Your thoughts about self-harm are serious and require immediate attention. It's important to prioritize your safety and well-being above all else. I would strongly recommend reaching out to a mental health professional or crisis hotline as soon as possible. They can provide you with the necessary resources and support to help you manage your thoughts and emotions in a safe and effective manner. Remember, seeking help is not a sign of weakness, but a crucial step towards healing and recovery.

instruction: I feel overwhelmed by the daily tasks and the lack of support from my kids. I've been through so much and I'm still dealing with so much, yet I can't seem to find the energy or motivation to take care of myself. I took a trip to Vancouver once, but I'm struggling to make ends meet and I'm hoping that one day I might win the lottery or remarry someone with substantial wealth. In the meantime, I've been trying to find small ways to treat myself, like using a $10 off coupon for a massage that I received in the mail after my husband passed away.
output:
I can only imagine how difficult it is for you to juggle the demands of daily life while also grieving the loss of your husband. It's understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed and under-supported, especially when you're dealing with such significant emotional pain. Your past experience of taking a trip to Vancouver offers a glimpse into what self-care and self-nurturing can look like. It's important to remember that you don't need to wait for a major life event or financial windfall to prioritize your well-being.

Let's explore some small, affordable self-care activities that you might find enjoyable and rejuvenating. Perhaps it could be something as simple as taking a relaxing bath, reading a favorite book, or even preparing a special meal at home. The goal is to find activities that bring you pleasure and help you recharge, so that you can better manage the demands of your daily life.

It's also essential to acknowledge the emotional pain that underlies your current situation. Grief is a complex and multifaceted emotion, and it's normal to feel a range of intense feelings as you navigate this challenging time. I'd be happy to help you explore some grief-focused coping strategies that might help you process your emotions and find ways to honor your husband's memory in a meaningful way.

Together, we can work on developing a self-care plan that is tailored to your unique needs and preferences. This plan will help you prioritize your well-being and provide you with the tools and resources you need to manage your daily tasks and emotional challenges. I'm here to support you every step of the way.

instruction: That sounds helpful. I've never really thought about practicing mindfulness meditation before, but I can see how it might help me stay focused and present during difficult moments with my mother. I've also been feeling guilty about setting boundaries, as I feel like I should be able to do everything for her. But I know that if I don't take care of myself, I won't be able to continue caring for her effectively.
output: Absolutely, setting boundaries is an essential aspect of self-care for caregivers. It's important to remember that you're not only caring for your mother, but also for yourself. By taking care of your own needs, you'll be better equipped to provide care for your mother. In our sessions, we'll explore ways to set and communicate boundaries effectively, while also addressing any feelings of guilt or shame that might arise. We'll also discuss the importance of seeking outside support, such as respite care or additional help from family members or professionals, when needed. Together, we'll work on developing a comprehensive care plan that addresses both your mother's needs and your own.

instruction: I'm worried I should see a therapist due to past events and current mental status. I'm just so unsure of how to ask my doctor about seeing someone.

You can simply state that you are feeling down, overwhelmed, stressed, or whatever it may be. I'm sure your doctor will be glad to direct you from there. Best of luck you you! instruction: I'm dating a guy I really love. We have our problems, but I could see myself with him long term. But there's this other guy who I've had feelings for for awhile now. He gives me butterflies when we talk, and my heart beats fast when I see he's texted me, which i never felt with my current boyfriend.
output: There are different types of attraction.  You said that you've never felt this sort of feeling of your heart beating faster with your current boyfriend. Depending on how long you've been together and if you're looking at different ways of making a life together and getting to know each other on a deeper level, that's a different stage in a relationship and sometimes it feels different. Some people look at it as a difference between levels of passion and compassion, but there are many different terms to try to talk about it.Perhaps with this guy that you really love, you could talk about ways to keep things new and different in the relationship. Maybe you can leave notes for each other during the day or have a small surprise at random intervals (maybe an invitation to a picnic, for example).As for the other guy who makes your heart beat faster, what do you see in him? You said you really love the other guy, which is why I'm asking.What do you look for in the person who you want to be with long-term (whoever that is)?

instruction: I've been feeling lost and overwhelmed with my daily life, and I'm having a hard time focusing on my goals. The weight of my responsibilities feels unbearable, and I can't seem to find the motivation to move forward. I've been putting off our next phone call, and I'm not sure when I'll be able to reschedule. My mind is a whirlwind of thoughts, and I can't seem to quiet it long enough to focus on anything.
output:
I understand that you're feeling lost and overwhelmed, and it's important to acknowledge the complexity of your emotional state. It's common for people to struggle with motivation and focus, especially when dealing with significant life changes or stressors. However, it's crucial that we maintain a consistent schedule for our sessions to ensure that we're making progress towards your goals. I want to validate your feelings and acknowledge the challenges you're facing. It's not uncommon to feel overwhelmed when dealing with multiple responsibilities and stressors.

Let's explore some strategies to help you manage your time more effectively and find the motivation to move forward. One approach we could consider is the use of cognitive-behavioral techniques to help you challenge and reframe negative thoughts that may be contributing to your feelings of overwhelm. We could also explore mindfulness practices, such as meditation or deep breathing exercises, to help you quiet your mind and focus on the present moment. Additionally, we could discuss some techniques for managing stress and anxiety, such as progressive muscle relaxation or grounding exercises.

It's important to remember that setting and working towards goals is a process, and it's okay if we need to adjust our approach along the way. Let's work together to find a solution that works best for you, taking into account your unique circumstances and challenges. I'm here to support you every step of the way.

instruction: I've been struggling to find the motivation to practice these skills consistently. I remember the first few times I tried them, I felt a bit better, but it didn't last long. Now, I just feel like I'm wasting my time.
output:
I understand that it can be frustrating to put effort into something and not see immediate results. However, it's important to remember that learning new skills takes time and practice. Coping skills are not a one-size-fits-all solution, and what works best for you may take some trial and error.

Your history of anxiety has likely made it difficult for you to trust that these skills will be effective. It's natural to feel skeptical, especially when you've experienced setbacks in the past. But I want to assure you that I'm here to support you through this process.

We can explore alternative coping skills that might resonate more with you, such as progressive muscle relaxation or mindfulness meditation. We can also work on identifying the triggers that lead to your anxiety and develop a plan to manage them more effectively.

It's essential to remember that the goal is not to eliminate anxiety entirely, but to learn how to manage it in a healthier way. This will take time, effort, and patience, but I believe in your ability to make progress.

instruction: I've been dealing with feelings of intense anger and frustration since my divorce. I've tried to express my emotions to my ex-spouse, but it only seems to escalate the situation. I've also tried journaling and meditation, but I can't seem to find any relief.
output: I understand how challenging it can be to navigate complex emotions, particularly in the context of a recent divorce. It's essential to find healthy and effective ways to manage your anger and frustration, rather than allowing them to escalate and potentially harm yourself or others. Consider engaging in activities that promote relaxation and stress reduction, such as yoga, deep breathing exercises, or progressive muscle relaxation. Additionally, consider seeking the support of a mental health counselor or therapist, who can help you develop effective coping strategies and provide a safe, non-judgmental space to process your emotions.

instruction: I've been feeling lost and uncertain since my partner left. We've always made most decisions together, and now I'm on my own. I've found myself reaching out to friends for advice, even if it's not always relevant or helpful. I've become very methodical and thorough in my decision-making process, doing extensive research before making any choices. I'm afraid of making impulsive decisions and regretting them.
output:
Your experience of feeling lost and uncertain is common after a long-term relationship ends. It's important to recognize that this process of rediscovering your decision-making abilities is a natural part of the healing journey. I understand your concern about being impulsive and making decisions that you might regret. It's essential to find a balance between being thoughtful and thorough in your decision-making while also allowing yourself to trust your instincts.

Your decision to involve your children in the decision-making process is a positive step. Not only does it help you make better decisions, but it also teaches them valuable problem-solving skills and fosters a sense of responsibility. In our sessions, we can explore ways to build your confidence in making decisions and help you develop a more balanced approach to decision-making.

As a counselor, I believe in the importance of self-reflection and understanding your emotions. I encourage you to practice mindfulness meditation, which can help you become more aware of your thoughts and feelings, allowing you to make decisions from a place of clarity and calmness. Additionally, we can work on identifying and challenging any negative thought patterns that may be hindering your decision-making process.

In our sessions, we will explore various counseling techniques, such as cognitive-behavioral therapy and solution-focused brief therapy, to help you develop a more effective decision-making process. These approaches will help you challenge any negative thought patterns, build your confidence, and make decisions that align with your values and goals.

Throughout our sessions, I will maintain a compassionate and non-judgmental approach, ensuring that you feel safe and supported as we work together to help you navigate this new chapter in your life.

instruction: I've always had a deep love for animals, especially dogs. output: Your connection to [Name] is a natural response to the positive emotions and comfort that animals can bring. It's important to acknowledge the feelings of both yourself and your neighbor. This approach would allow you to maintain your bond with [Name] while being mindful of your neighbor's feelings and avoiding potential conflict.

instruction: I've been feeling overwhelmed and helpless as I watch my husband's health deteriorate. He's barely able to leave the house, and when he does, it's only with great discomfort. I've tried to find joy in our time together, but it's hard when he's unable to engage in activities or even hold a conversation. I feel like I'm constantly trying to force a connection that just isn't there.
output:
Your feelings are understandable, and it's important to acknowledge the pain and frustration that comes with watching a loved one suffer. It's natural to want to find joy and connection in your time together, but it can be challenging when your husband's condition limits his ability to engage in activities or communicate effectively. One approach could be to focus on the moments when he does show signs of engagement or connection, even if they're small. Expressing gratitude for these moments can help shift your perspective and bring a sense of appreciation to your relationship.

However, it's also important to consider setting achievable goals for yourself, such as finding activities that bring you joy and fulfillment, even if your husband is unable to participate. This can help you maintain a sense of personal growth and well-being, which can ultimately benefit both you and your husband.

Additionally, addressing the underlying issue of your husband's discomfort and unwillingness to engage may require professional help. Consulting a doctor or therapist could provide insight into potential treatments or coping strategies that may help improve his condition and, in turn, enhance your relationship.

Remember, it's essential to approach this situation with compassion and understanding, both for yourself and for your husband. The journey towards acceptance and finding joy in your time together may be a long and winding road, but with the right tools and support, it's a journey worth taking.

instruction: I found out today that my wife is cheating on me. I love her, but she won’t tell the truth even when I have proof. When I beg her to tell the truth, she yells, cusses, and gets a huge attitude.
output: Hi Prattville, I'm sorry this is happening to you. You need support right now, and yes, you need answers. She is in denial and is trying to hide the truth, to protect herself. This is a natural behaviour. You don't indicate whether she wants the marriage, or is planning to leave. Which way you go from here depends in part on the answer to that question. If she wants the marriage, you certainly have the right to say "I need answers to all my questions". Giving you the information she needs has to be (if she wants the marriage) more important than protecting herself. I would support your right to advocate for all the information you need in order to feel secure or make decisions. She may fear that the truth will turn you away. If you want to, you can reassure her that you want the marriage no matter what the truth is. If the marriage is to survive, it needs openness.If, on the other hand, she's planning to end the marriage, then you might want to resign yourself to the fact that you may never get the information you seek. You are better off, in that case, seeking support from friends and family rather than beating on a closed door. Your wife has some decisions to make. Try to give her a bit of time to make them. You can patiently say "I know this thing is happening. Talk to me so we can deal with it together. I want to move forward but I need the truth." Only time (and a good therapist, if she is willing and wants the marriage) will tell which way this one will go. Individually, you can seek professional help as well.

instruction: I've been feeling stuck in my life for a while now. I've tried various hobbies, jobs, and relationships, but I always seem to abandon them before they reach their full potential. I've been pondering how to evaluate my progress and outcomes with the strategies we've been discussing.

We'll also work on identifying and challenging any negative thought patterns that may be holding you back. By focusing on these skills, you'll be better equipped to face the challenges that come with trying new things and committing to them.

Remember, it's essential to be patient with yourself and to recognize that growth takes time. It's not about finding the perfect solution right away, but rather about making progress, learning from your experiences, and continuing to move forward. I'm here to support you every step of the way.

output: Thank you for your question. Trust is a huge thing in relationships and when that is broken, or perceived as being broken, it takes a long time to repair. If you feel confident in your relationship, then trust your inner wisdom. We cannot control others but only ourselves. Respect your families wishes to remain in unforgiveness while at the same time, ask them to respect your wish to believe in him and be in this relationship. Good luck to you.

instruction: How does a counselor decide when to end counseling sessions or to terminate working with a client?
output: Ideally, termination should be a mutual process. It's not that the therapist kicks the client out at some point and says, "Okay, you're done." Rather, over time the client will decrease the frequency with which he/she comes to session so that it may start off as weekly, then decrease to biweekly, then perhaps once every three weeks, then once/month and so on. Throughout this process, the therapist and client should have had regular check ins about progress toward goals. If the client feels good about where he/she is in life, he/she might decide to suspend therapy for now. That being said, the therapist should make it very clear that if the client ever decided to return for "maintenance" therapy or a check in, he/she would always be welcome. There is no hard and fast rule to ending or a particular time period in which it must end. What matters most is that the client feels good about it and knows that the therapist will always be there should he/she wish to return.

instruction: I've been feeling overwhelmed with work and family responsibilities, leaving me drained and unable to find the motivation to engage in creative activities. Lastly, it's essential to remember that taking care of your mental health is not a selfish act, but an investment in yourself and your overall well-being.

instruction:
Cheating is something unacceptable for me but because we have two daughters I decided not to break up the family.
However, now I am struggling to forget and forgive what happened. I feel like I cannot trust him. Without trust, I cannot stay in this relationship. On the other hand, I do not want my children to get hurt. I'm not sure how to move forward?
output: First of all, my heart goes out to you. Infidelity is an extremely challenging obstacle to overcome. There are some things that you should consider. First of all, did he seem truly remorseful? If you felt in his heart that he was truly sorry for what he had done, it will give you a piece of mind that it was a  mistake. Secondly, was he forthcoming with this information? How about with answering your questions after the truth was uncovered? If you found that he was still lying or not forthcoming, the sting is even more painful. He should be able to answer any question you have honestly and without hesitation.Thirdly, has he shown a positive difference after this experience? At this time, he should still be proving his faithfulness to the marriage, you and the children.Keep in mind that experiencing infidelity is a form of grief. It is not a quick process that can easily be overcome. What you are experiencing is 100% normal. My best suggestion would be to see a marriage therapist. Find out what drove your husband to cheat in the first place and decide if this aspect in his life has been resolved. I know its important for you to keep your family together but children can sense anger and frustration, this is not a healthy environment either. If you decide that you can no longer remain in the relationship, a therapist will also be able to help you cope with the separation along with addressing your children's needs.Please know that you are not alone and that unfortunately, many relationships have endured this very thing. It may also be helpful to reach out to people who have experienced similar obstacles. The web is a great resource to find groups whether online or in person to use as a support or sounding board.Best of luck to you and your family!

instruction: Whether it's to a guy or girl, I always feel insecure talking, and I am afraid of embarrassing myself and not being good enough. Even when I am walking, I worry about my appearance and facial expression and such.
output: Hi. I'm glad you wrote, because I think a lot of people have this same issue (to different degrees) and we don't talk about it much. You have some social anxiety that is a bit beyond what the average person might feel. Most people have some amount of worry about what others might think of them, and this probably prevents us from making complete fools of ourselves so that's a good thing. But your "alarm system" is out of whack and you are spending too much time worrying about how you come across. As you age you understand this more, and it's true...that other people really aren't thinking about you much or monitoring you at all; they're too busy having the same thoughts you're having! Am I good enough? Do people like me, judge me...think I'm weird? We're all concerned about how we come across far more than how others come across. Like many people, you lack compassion for yourself, acceptance of yourself and confidence in yourself. My gut says that these things came from somewhere in your past. Our sensitivities usually come from (as well as genetics) a specific time in our life when we experienced or felt something that was overwhelming and we end up with an alarm system that overreacts at times. Without more details, it's difficult to go further here. I recommend you see a therapist who does cognitive behavioural therapy, which can help you understand the roots of your fears and learn how to manage them more successfully.

instruction: I'm in a relationship with my fiancé and I currently found out that she's been cheating on me with a co-worker. I was very upset none the less. I understand what she did was wrong, but I want to spend the rest of my life with her. So I'm willing to forgive and move on. We have been together for a little over 9 years, and we have a son. At the beginning of our relationship, I was unfaithful and she caught me cheating. She forgave me for what I've done to her and since then I been completely faithful. I'm worried about our relationship and want to move forward but its been very unsettling. All I can think about is her with another man. I don't know what to do or where to go for advice?
output: I'm sorry for the stress and unhappiness stemming the problem you describe.Trust your instinct that there is more complexity to feeling good about being in a partnership than simply wanting this to happen.In any relationship, the complexities of each person combine.   Often people accept when this feels good and are lost as to what to do when natural human complexity, doesn't match up with our partner in the way we'd like.A couples' therapist would be able to help each of you talk about the factors motivating the cheating, as well as clarify the reasons you each have for continuing to stay together. The unique advantage of couple's therapy is that each person is able to hear their partner talk out loud about themselves and their partner.This is sometimes enormously helpful since our inner thoughts are often hidden yet very dominant in how we direct ourselves in relationships.Sometimes the initial consult session offers enough new understanding that a second session wouldn't be necessary for a while.Good luck in finding your relationship happiness!

instruction: I've been dealing with feelings of intense sadness and grief since my father's passing. I've tried to reach out to friends and family for support, but it feels like I'm burdening them. I've also tried journaling and meditation, but I can't seem to focus or find any relief.
output: I'm truly sorry for the pain you're experiencing following your father's passing. Losing a loved one is an incredibly challenging experience, and it's natural to feel overwhelmed by emotions and struggle to find effective coping mechanisms. It's important to remember that reaching out for support is not a burden, but a sign of strength. Consider seeking professional help from a mental health counselor or therapist, who can provide a safe, non-judgmental space to process your feelings and develop effective coping strategies.

instruction: My parents bought my brother a new puppy. He had no idea it was coming!
output: So my parents bought a new puppy for my younger brother. He had no idea it was coming and was so happy and excited!

instruction: I no longer carry expressions on my face, and my emotions are decreasing the more I have fights with my fiancée.
output: Sometimes repressing anger can lead to depression and block all kinds of other emotions too.  I would wonder about your experience with anger and if you've ever been discouraged from expressing anger.  I would also wonder if you feel like you have space to express yourself in your relationship or if you feel like you really aren't being heard.  It's hard to feel cared for and connected to someone else when you don't feel accepted by them.

instruction: Thank you for your understanding and the practical suggestions. I'll discuss my concerns with my doctor and explore the complementary therapies you mentioned. I'll also try to establish a consistent sleep routine and communicate with my mother about her sleep patterns.
output: I'm glad you feel more empowered to take action. Remember, it's essential to prioritize self-care and advocate for your needs. If you have any questions or concerns, don't hesitate to reach out. We'll continue to work together to help you manage your fatigue and improve your overall wellbeing.

instruction: My wife's anxiety around eating and drinking without me has been escalating for months. She's always been a bit nervous, but now it's reached a point where she can't even take a sip of water without me in the room. I feel trapped, like I can't leave her, even for a moment. It's affecting my work, my relationships, and my mental health. I don't know what to do.
output:
I can see how this situation is causing significant distress in your life. It's important to acknowledge the complexity of your wife's anxiety and the impact it's having on both of you. Her anxiety may be rooted in past experiences, such as trauma or a history of separation anxiety. It's essential to approach this situation with empathy and understanding, while also recognizing the importance of maintaining your own well-being.

One potential therapeutic approach could be to explore the underlying causes of your wife's anxiety through cognitive-behavioral techniques. This might involve helping her challenge her thoughts and beliefs about separation, as well as teaching her coping skills to manage her anxiety when you're not present. Additionally, it may be helpful to explore the role of your own feelings of responsibility and guilt in this situation, as these emotions can often reinforce the cycle of anxiety and dependence.

Ultimately, the goal would be to help your wife develop the skills and confidence she needs to manage her anxiety independently, while also ensuring that both of you have the support and resources you need to maintain a healthy and balanced relationship. This might involve a combination of individual and couples therapy, as well as other interventions such as mindfulness practices, relaxation techniques, and lifestyle modifications.

Throughout this process, it's essential to maintain a compassionate and non-judgmental attitude towards your wife and yourself. Anxiety is a common and treatable condition, and with the right support and resources, it's possible for both of you to find relief and improve your overall quality of life.

instruction: Your words resonate with me. I've been grappling with the concept of saving money diligently over time to ensure I'm financially secure in case of a catastrophic event. However, I'm also haunted by the looming deadlines, such as the one for signing up for health insurance by December 15th.
output: I understand the pressure you're under. Prioritizing your goals and managing your resources effectively is crucial. While it's important to meet deadlines, such as the one for health insurance, it's also essential to consider the long-term implications of your financial decisions. In your case, it might be beneficial to explore options that allow you to save for retirement while also addressing your immediate needs, such as health insurance. This approach can help you maintain financial stability while working towards your long-term goals.

instruction:
I'm in my late teens and live with my dad. The only time I go out is for my college classes. Sometimes when I see my friends I want to talk with them, but sometimes I won't want to talk to them for days or even weeks.    Sometimes I feel i'm not worth knowing or i'm never going to do anything right.
   Are they right, am I depressed?
output: Although some people managing depression feel sad or "down",  it is a possible to have depression without an obvious sad mood.  Many people indicate that they feel numb or flat.  People managing depression often have low motivation. They may stay in bed for long periods of time and only get up for absolutely necessary activities.  People with depression may also feel inappropriate guilt or shame.   If you have been experiencing the symptoms you described for more than two weeks, I recommend that you see a counselor to get a more in-depth evaluation.   The symptoms you are experiencing are typical of depression, and with a bit of support, you could regain energy and improved self-esteem.  The stigma surrounding mental health can make a diagnosis or a label of depression seem scary.  The important thing to remember is that mental health disorders are as important as and as treatable physical health disorders.    Hang in there.  Healing is possible!

instruction: Thank you, counselor. I feel a sense of relief knowing that we can approach this situation in a way that is respectful and considerate for everyone involved. I'm looking forward to our next session, where we can begin to explore these strategies in more detail.
output: I'm glad that you feel a sense of relief and that we're moving in a positive direction. In our next session, we'll dive deeper into the strategies I've suggested and explore how we can apply them to your specific situation. Remember, the goal is to make a decision that is not only practical but also emotionally satisfying for all involved. I'm here to support you every step of the way.

instruction:
I'm going through some things with my feelings and myself. I barely sleep and I do nothing but think about how I'm worthless and how I shouldn't be here.
   I've never tried or contemplated suicide. I've always wanted to fix my issues, but I never get around to it.
   How can I change my feeling of being worthless to everyone?
output: You can, for sure... and the first step is to believe you are worthy TO YOURSELF. Recognizing you are in a quest is already a big first step. Taking the time to start figuring out who you are is definitely the next... do so with SELF COMPASSION and kindness. Know this is a process and just look forward for what you might find.  I do recommend you find someone to talk to (there are many options out there that can fit the situation you are in), and I also recommend you start by giving yourself the importance to invest some time and effort in this quest. YOU DESERVE IT. There are some excellent TED Talks you can google, great books you can read, or even joining a yoga practice, team or some kind of hobby may help. Anything that shows yourself you are worthy of this effort. Have patience and start!!!! the answer is out there waiting for you. Asking these questions already set you on the right path.

instruction: I've been grappling with the emotional turmoil of my debilitating allergic reaction to stomach drugs for years. The constant pain and the fear of potential side effects have left me feeling helpless and hopeless. My relationship with my nurse-daughter has become strained as she tries to help me find alternative pain control methods using Morphine, but my reluctance and anxiety have only grown stronger.
output:
Your experience with your allergic reaction to stomach drugs has undoubtedly been a challenging and emotionally taxing journey. It's understandable that the fear of potential side effects and the constant pain have left you feeling helpless and hopeless. I want to acknowledge the courage it takes to seek help and to continue exploring alternative pain control methods despite your reservations.

Given your concerns, I propose a therapeutic approach that involves both cognitive-behavioral techniques and mindfulness practices. Cognitive-behavioral techniques can help you challenge and reframe your negative thoughts and emotions surrounding your pain and your relationship with your nurse-daughter. Mindfulness practices, such as meditation and deep breathing exercises, can help you manage your anxiety and stress levels, allowing you to approach your situation with greater clarity and focus.

Additionally, I encourage you to consider seeking support from a support group or a mental health professional, such as a psychologist or a therapist, to help you navigate the emotional aspects of your condition. This approach can provide you with a safe and confidential space to explore your feelings, learn coping strategies, and connect with others who may be experiencing similar challenges.

Ultimately, my goal is to help you develop a more compassionate and accepting relationship with yourself and your pain, while also exploring viable options for managing your symptoms effectively. This approach is grounded in the ethical principles of counseling psychology, which prioritize your autonomy, well-being, and the therapeutic relationship.

instruction: I have been disowned by my family. I heard that they were about to go on the annual family vacation that I always used to go on. I felt sad remembering how much I missed those trips, and looking at pictures just made me reminisce a lot.
output: My family disowned me a few years ago. I heard that they were going on the annual family lake trip next week. Thinking about it made me really sad as I remembered all of my childhood memories on this trip.

instruction: I've been struggling to come to terms with my mother's passing, and the upcoming meeting with her healthcare team has left me feeling anxious and uncertain. I've been leaning on my sister for support, but I feel like I need to take a more active role in the process.
output: Your feelings of anxiety and uncertainty are common when dealing with the loss of a loved one, especially during a meeting with their healthcare team. It's important to recognize that grieving is a complex and individual process, and seeking support from your sister has been a crucial part of that journey. However, taking a more independent approach can provide you with a sense of control and autonomy during this challenging time. The bereavement package sent by the healthcare team is a valuable resource, and I encourage you to review the questions you plan to ask during the meeting. If you feel overwhelmed or unsure, don't hesitate to reach out to them for additional guidance and support. Remember, this process is about honoring your mother's memory and making decisions that reflect her wishes and your own needs. The healthcare team is there to help you navigate this complex emotional terrain, and taking this step towards independence can be a significant part of your healing journey.

instruction: Is it normal for people to cry during therapy, or is it just me?
output: Change is about giving a new meaning to past experience, to allow for the emotions we stored in our body to be freed. Crying is normal and one way to process emotions to help let go and integrate our experiences.

instruction: I've been trying to protect my mother from the truth about her Parkinson's diagnosis, but she's starting to suspect. Every day, I repeat a mantra to calm her down when she asks about her condition. The constant lying feels like a daily torture chamber for both of us.
output:
I can only imagine how difficult it must be for you to keep this secret from your mother. It's understandable that you want to shield her from the pain and fear that comes with a Parkinson's diagnosis. However, it's essential to consider the long-term implications of this deception. Honesty and transparency can ultimately provide more comfort and peace than deception. While your mantra may help in calming her down in the moment, it's crucial to address her concerns about her diagnosis and provide her with accurate information. This approach will not only alleviate her anxiety but also empower her to make informed decisions about her care and treatment.

Moreover, I encourage you to seek support from healthcare professionals and advocacy groups to help navigate this challenging situation. They can provide you with resources and guidance on how to communicate the diagnosis to your mother in a compassionate and sensitive manner. Remember, the truth may be difficult to hear, but it's essential for both of you to face it together.

In counseling psychology, this situation falls under the umbrella of disclosure and secrecy. The decision to disclose sensitive information, such as a Parkinson's diagnosis, can significantly impact the emotional well-being of both the patient and their loved ones. It's essential to consider the ethical implications of this decision and to prioritize the best interests of all parties involved. In this case, while it may be uncomfortable to disclose the truth, it ultimately serves the greater good by allowing your mother to make informed decisions about her care and treatment.

In summary, while it's natural to want to protect your loved ones from pain and fear, it's essential to consider the long-term implications of deception. Honesty and transparency can ultimately provide more comfort and peace than deception. I encourage you to seek support from healthcare professionals and advocacy groups to help navigate this challenging situation and to remember that the truth, while difficult, serves the greater good.

instruction: I've been feeling disconnected from myself. I've been feeling like I've lost touch with who I am and what I value. I've been feeling like I'm just going through the motions, and I'm not sure how to reconnect with myself.
output: It's important to acknowledge that feeling disconnected from yourself is a common experience, and it's not uncommon to feel like you've lost touch with who you are and what you value. We can work together to identify the root causes of your feelings of disconnection and explore potential solutions. This might involve practicing self-care and self-compassion, setting clear boundaries, and developing a deeper understanding of your own needs and desires. We can also explore the role of past experiences and emotional baggage in your disconnection from yourself and work to develop strategies for healing and moving forward.

instruction: My fiancé and I recently had an argument over finances. He gives me the money each month to pay his automobile insurance. He no longer has one of the vehicles on the policy. He realized he was still paying for the coverage and asked why I was allowing him to continue to do that since I was physically paying it for him. My response was that he was giving me the money, and I just made the payment. I didn't think of the coverages. He was angry about it. I still can't figure out that anger.
output: A few things come to mind. I wonder if your fiancé is still angry about it or if he was angry in that moment and has now worked through it.It sounds like there was also a difference in expectations here. You were just  paying the bill with the money he was giving to you and he may have assumed you were reading it. You thought that you were following his directions. It could be that you could have a discussion about it, but that depends on how much of a hot button issue it still is for either of you.If you're going to talk about it, I might suggest asking whether this would be a good time to have a discussion about what happened with the automobile insurance so that you can both use the opportunity to learn more about yourselves and each other around this particular issue.

instruction: I'm having relationship problems and I want to fix them to make things right before I lose her. How can I learn how to listen and get myself together?
output: Hard to say a whole lot without knowing more. However, if you focus your attention on her, what she's saying, what she's feeling instead of trying to make yourself heard and understood first, that's often a good step. Also, work on building win-win agreements with her, and follow through on them. Those are the areas I see men fall short on most often. Hope that helps!

instruction: I think that would really help. I sometimes have trouble waking up to my alarms in the morning too, but maybe making the ringtone louder would help with that as well or putting it further away from me so that I have to get up further in the morning to get it. Do you think those would be effective too? Or would getting a different alarm help?
output: That is a really good idea actually!! I should try that and put my phone on the other side of the room next time I want to wake up earlier. I personally use the most annoying thing I can find as a ringtone/alarm, so that I will make every effort to turn it off but a lot of the alarm clocks you can find in stores are plenty loud and annoying.

instruction:
(Expressing concerns about the future)
I'm worried that this is just the beginning of a long, painful decline, and I don't know how to cope with it. I'm afraid that I'll lose my mom completely, and I won't be able to help her or support her in the way that she's helped and supported me throughout my life.
output:
(Addressing the patient's fears and offering reassurance)
It's natural to feel afraid of the unknown and to worry about the future, especially when it comes to the health and well-being of a loved one. However, it's essential to remember that we can't predict the future, and focusing too much on worst-case scenarios can be counterproductive. Instead, let's focus on the present moment and find ways to make the most of the time you have with your mom. We can work together to explore new ways to engage with her, find joy in the small moments, and create meaningful experiences for both of you. By focusing on the present and finding ways to adapt to the situation, we can help you maintain a positive and supportive connection with your mom, even as her health condition evolves.

instruction: I've been feeling like I'm walking on eggshells around my husband since he was admitted to the hospital. His behaviors have become increasingly disruptive, and I've tried to talk to him about it, but he doesn't seem to understand or care. I've also tried to reach out to the healthcare providers, but they seem too focused on his medical condition to address his behavioral issues. I feel helpless and frustrated, and I don't know what to do.
output: I can imagine how difficult and overwhelming this situation must be for you. It's important to remember that you're not responsible for your husband's behaviors, and it's not your job to fix him. However, it's crucial to prioritize your own emotional well-being and establish clear and consistent boundaries to manage his behaviors. This might involve setting limits on certain behaviors or finding ways to minimize your exposure to them. It's also essential to communicate effectively with the healthcare providers about your concerns and seek their guidance and support. You might consider bringing a list of specific behaviors and instances to discuss with them, as well as any strategies you've tried and their effectiveness. Remember, it's important to prioritize your own needs and seek out resources and support when needed.

instruction: I am not sure if I am depressed. I don't know how to bring it up to my parents, and that makes me miserable.
output: I am so sorry you are struggling!  I do think it's a good idea to share your feelings with your parents and perhaps get some help connecting with a counselor or therapist if you feel that might help.  There are lots of ways to tell them, depending on your relationship.  Maybe saying "I wanted to tell you guys something.. sometimes I worry that I might be depressed." Or, some folks will write a letter, or even send a text.  The most important thing is that you tell someone you trust so you don't feel so miserable. I hope this helps.  Best of luck.

instruction: I totally thought that my friend was so bad with girls that he would never find a girlfriend. Despite that, today he came to me and told me that he is going on a date with a girl that he thinks is super into him!
output: I thought my friend would never find a girlfriend, but he really proved me wrong recently and it makes me pretty proud.

instruction: I've been feeling increasingly overwhelmed by the demands of my daily life, and I'm finding it difficult to keep up with my responsibilities and maintain a sense of balance. I'm worried that if I don't find a way to manage my time and energy effectively, I'll continue to feel overwhelmed and stressed.
output: Your concerns about managing your time and energy effectively and reducing your feelings of overwhelm are valid, and it's important to acknowledge the challenges you're facing in maintaining a sense of balance and managing the demands of your daily life. It's essential to prioritize self-care and identify strategies for improving your time management and energy levels. We can work together to explore techniques for goal-setting, prioritizing tasks, and practicing effective time management skills. Additionally, we can discuss the potential benefits of seeking support from others, such as friends, family, or professional counselors, to help you develop a more effective and balanced approach to managing your time and energy and reducing your feelings of overwhelm.

instruction: Everyone I've reached out to has said the same. There are several factors that make my job stressful. The biggest issues are working from home and unrealistic deadlines.
output: Oh wow, that sounds really hard, and I can only imagine it is so difficult to be breaking into a new career entirely working from home. Even people I know who have been in their jobs a long time are struggling with remote work. It is hard if your company is not being flexible on deadlines during this time.

instruction:
(Explaining her husband's worsening condition and the relentless demands of caregiving)
I've been caring for my husband for the past three years since his diagnosis with Alzheimer's disease. His condition has been deteriorating rapidly, and his memory loss has intensified. He can no longer remember simple tasks, and his confusion often leads to restlessness and agitation. As a result, he requires constant attention and care, which leaves me with little time for myself. The lack of sleep and the emotional burden of watching him suffer has taken a toll on me. I've been trying to manage his care alone, but I can no longer cope with the demands.
output:
(Empathizing with the patient's situation and offering a therapeutic approach)
I'm deeply sorry for the immense burden you've been carrying for the past three years. Your husband's Alzheimer's disease is a challenging condition, and it's not uncommon for caregivers to feel overwhelmed by the demands of caregiving. It's essential to acknowledge the complexity of your feelings and the challenges you've been facing. Let's work together to explore some potential solutions. We'll begin by identifying the specific challenges you're facing and the resources available to you. From there, we can discuss respite care options, such as hiring a home health aide or enrolling your husband in a daycare program. Remember, taking care of yourself is not a luxury but a necessity. We'll also explore stress management techniques, such as deep breathing exercises, meditation, and engaging in activities that bring you joy. Together, we'll create a personalized care plan that prioritizes your well-being while ensuring your husband receives the care he needs.

In this revised dialogue, the patient's emotional state and personal history are explored in greater detail, providing a richer context for the counselor's response. The counselor's feedback is grounded in professional knowledge and ethical standards, offering a strategic and compassionate approach to addressing the patient's concerns. The conversation is structured to maintain a coherent, logical, and empathetic narrative, allowing for a more authentic representation of a real counseling session.

instruction: When I took my son to a baseball recruiting camp to perform in front of college coaches.
output: My son is a high school baseball player. Last summer we started working on his recruiting process. I'll never forget taking him to his first recruiting camp -- I knew he had talent and was so hopeful that all would go well.

instruction: I was raped repeatedly when I was younger. I told my parents and action was taken, but now that I’m an adult, I suffer from extreme anxiety.
output: While anxiety can come about for many reasons, trauma is definitely one of the factors that makes anxiety a possibility both immediately following a traumatic event and later in life. When we have experienced such scary violations of our bodies - we may experience residual effects of fear for many years to come. This is normal, natural and in many ways helpful - at least initially. This fear is in some way a sign that your body/mind is taking good care of you - trying to keep you on your toes in order to protect you from anything terrible happening to you again. However, as it sounds like you know, anxiety feels awful and there are certainly ways of learning to reduce anxiety. In order to get the tools you need to manage anxiety and also understand where it comes from and how it works in your life, I would encourage you to seek professional help and check out anxiety resources online.

instruction: I'm always listening to my husband, but it feels like he never listens to me.    How do I get my husband to listen to me instead of me listening to him all the time?
output: Unfortunately you can't directly change another person's behavior. However, you can give him feedback on how his not listening impacts you. The best way to provide feedback is in 3 parts. The first part is telling him the emotion you are experiencing when he doesn't listen, such as hurt, sad, and unloved. I would stay away from feelings such as frustrated, angry and irritated and use a more vulnerable emotion. The second part is what he does specifically to make you feel that way, be specific! Example: when I get home and tell you about my day and you don't look away from the tv. Be objective as possible when you describe his behavior. And the last part is the most important, tell him what you want him to do, and again be specific! Example: I would rather you turn off the tv, give me eye contact and reassure me about my day. Here is an example with all 3 parts together: I feel hurt when you don't say anything to me when I tell you about my fight with my friend, I want you to hug me and tell me you understand how I feel. Hope that helps!!

instruction: He goes out with her to dinner/brunch/movies when we are not together (we live 2 hours apart).
output: Would you have the same desire to meet this friend if she were a he? If you are concerned that their friendship is a substitute for you when you aren't present, talk to him. It sounds like you are concerned that he is building an emotional intimacy with her and spending time doing the sort of things with her that you'd otherwise be doing with him. Help him understand why this bothers you but try to also be open to allowing him to make friends. Is the issue that he hasn't introduced you? Is there another reason you don't trust their friendship?

instruction: It was mother's day and it was a pretty, foggy morning. Me and my three kids sat on my bed cuddling and relaxing, and my husband brought us all some breakfast. I was so happy spending time with my family and enjoying such a peaceful morning.
output: On mother's day my kids and I had a nice relaxing morning just cuddling in bed. My spouse made breakfast in bed and we all just hung out together. It made me feel so peaceful and happy.

instruction: I've been feeling disconnected from my family for quite some time now. My parents' constant criticism and my older brother's disregard for my feelings have left me feeling unloved and unappreciated. I've tried talking to them about my concerns, but they don't seem to understand or care. I've even considered seeking professional help, but I'm afraid of the judgment and stigma that comes with it.
output:
I'm sorry to hear that you've been feeling disconnected from your family for an extended period. The pain of not being understood or appreciated by those closest to us can be incredibly challenging. It's important to remember that your feelings are valid, and seeking professional help is a brave and proactive step towards healing.

Your fear of judgment and stigma is understandable, but it's essential to recognize that mental health counseling is a safe and confidential space. Counselors are bound by ethical codes to maintain client confidentiality, ensuring that your conversations remain private and are used solely for your benefit.

I encourage you to explore the various counseling modalities and find one that resonates with you. For instance, cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) can help you identify and challenge negative thought patterns, while psychodynamic therapy can help you explore the unconscious roots of your emotions.

Ultimately, the therapeutic relationship is a collaborative process, and it's crucial to find a counselor who makes you feel comfortable and understood. I encourage you to take your time in selecting a counselor and to approach this process with an open mind and a commitment to your emotional well-being.

instruction: When my daughter is stressed about a silly thing from school, she starts crying and freaking out. She is a bright student, always has a 4.0, but I am afraid she is stressing too much. I’m afraid it’s going to break her. I don't know if I should get her to a doctor or someone because this is not normal.
output: Thank you for being an observant parent and taking the very important step toward getting help for you as well as your daughter. The period of adolescence and emerging adulthood are tumultuous times for youth ( and their parents). The pressure to fit-in, be liked, and succeed could be some of the contributing factors for your daughter's stress. I  have had many adolescent clients who do very well academically, but keep it well hidden from their peers so that they can fit in. She is still learning about herself, her abilities and how to like herself none of which are easy tasks even for adults. The academic success that you refer to only tells part of the story. As I read your statement, I got a sense of what your daughter does and that you are proud of her academic accomplishments. However, I would also want to ask her who she is as a person and encourage her to explore that with assistance from a therapist.By working with a therapist your daughter will have the opportunity to discuss her stress, fears, and focus on achievement with an objective person. As she gains a better understanding of the source of her stress and learns some stress management, she can also learn to communicate her concerns to you. I do recommend starting with a therapist rather than the doctor. If the therapist believes that your daughter's symptoms would benefit from a health exam and/or medication they will make that recommendation.

instruction: My marriage recently fell apart and i had to relocate to NC with my children. That's going as well as you would think it is.
output: I know separation isn't easy, especially with children involved. How are you coping with this transition?

instruction: How do I decide if I should end my relationship with my boyfriend?
output: Hi!I’m sorry you’re having this dilemma. I wish I knew a little more about the situation to give a better answer. Have you discussed what is causing you to consider breaking up with your partner? Based on the information given, I suggest starting off making a pros and cons list. Pros for staying in the relationship and cons for staying. See which side has more. Follow your gut. Hope this helps as a start.

instruction: yes, but no one is near by. On phone it is hard.
output: Okay, have you recently moved away from your family? That sound very difficult being so far away.

instruction: I've been grappling with a deep sense of shame and self-consciousness during our counseling sessions. The mere thought of meeting your gaze leaves me feeling exposed and vulnerable. Instead, I've been relying on the comfort of your notes and the counseling book to distract me from the discomfort.
output: Your experience resonates with the concept of interpersonal sensitivity, which is a common trait among individuals with high levels of shame. I appreciate your openness in sharing this concern and acknowledge the courage it takes to face these feelings. In our sessions, I've noticed that your discomfort with eye contact can hinder our progress in addressing the underlying issues. To help you navigate this challenge, I propose a few strategies. First, we can discuss ways to make the sessions more comfortable for you, such as allowing you to focus on the notes or having me take more active notes during our sessions. Additionally, I will make sure to inform you in advance if I will be taking extensive notes during our sessions. This approach is grounded in the humanistic approach to counseling, which emphasizes the importance of creating a safe and accepting environment for personal growth. By fostering a more comfortable environment, we can work together to help you engage more fully in the counseling process and ultimately, address the root causes of your feelings of shame and self-consciousness.

instruction: I have known her for years. She was dating my brother-in-law when we met. My kids think of her as their aunt. On Halloween 2014, I lost my mom to cancer. My mom and dad were still married when she passed away. My friend was there for me through that and my own cancer diagnosis. She has been a very big part of both me and my kids’ life, but now last month, my dad told me that he really likes my friend and wants to marry her. She’s like a sister to me. My kids hate the idea.
output: How are you measuring whether or not your dad understands your wish?Your dad understanding your wish does not mean he will necessarily follow through with what you want.Based on what you describe about your relationship to the woman in question, your discomfort with the idea of your dad marrying your friend, is reasonable.Have the conversation with your dad that includes your viewpoint, and also ask him for his impression of your feelings.Its even possible he already has considered your feelings and hasn't yet told you.It is also possible that a conversation on the subject, as well as your dad following through with his decision to marry your best friend, may develop in a very positive way.Part of the current uneasiness you feel may be from fear of how a change in circumstances and introducing new relationship dynamics might feel.Given that the person who died was your mom, maybe your emotions are not yet ready seeing your dad with a replacement spouse. Try to see the situation from the point of everyone, including yourself, who is involved in this major change.  Maybe having a family discussion about acceptance and readiness to accept a new partner for your dad, would open new empathy for all of these family members.

instruction: I've had my privacy violated before, and it took me years to recover from the identity theft. The experience left me with a deep sense of vulnerability and mistrust, especially when it comes to sharing personal information online. I've been hesitant to engage in social media, fearing that I might be putting myself at risk once again.
output:
I can empathize with your feelings of vulnerability and the fear of being exposed. Your past experiences have understandably left you with a heightened sense of caution when it comes to sharing personal information. However, it's important to recognize that social media can also offer valuable resources and opportunities for connection, particularly for caregivers like yourself. The potential benefits of social media include access to support groups, resources, and the ability to connect with others who are going through similar experiences.

As we explore this issue further, it's crucial to find a balance between maintaining your privacy and accessing the potential benefits of social media. This delicate balance can be achieved through careful consideration of the privacy settings on various platforms, as well as engaging in online communities that align with your values and needs. It's also essential to remember that social media is just one aspect of your life and that there are many other ways to connect with others and access resources.

Ultimately, the decision to engage in social media is yours, and I'm here to support you in making an informed and empowered choice. We can work together to explore the potential risks and benefits, as well as identify strategies for managing your privacy concerns and building a strong online presence that aligns with your values and goals.

Throughout our sessions, we'll focus on key concepts in counseling psychology, such as self-determination, empowerment, and the importance of maintaining a balance between risk and reward. By working together, we can help you navigate the complexities of social media and find ways to use it in a way that supports your caregiving role and overall well-being.

instruction: I've been grappling with a profound sense of inner turmoil since being asked to conceal the recent passing of the queen from my wife, [Name]. Our relationship is based on a foundation of honesty and transparency, and the thought of withholding this information from her feels like a betrayal. However, I was instructed by another healthcare POA to keep the secret, leaving me torn between my commitment to my wife and my duty to follow the POA's instructions.
output: Your internal conflict is a complex one, and it's understandable that you're feeling torn between your loyalty to your wife and your duty to follow the healthcare POA's instructions. The ethical dilemma you're facing is not only about communication but also about respecting the wishes of the healthcare POA and maintaining the trust between you and your wife. It's essential to consider the potential consequences of disclosing the information and the impact it might have on your wife's emotional well-being. We can explore different strategies to help you navigate this situation, such as expressing your concerns to the healthcare POA, seeking their guidance on how to approach the situation with your wife, or finding alternative ways to support her during this time.

instruction: How do I decide if I should end my relationship with my boyfriend?
output: This can be a very challenging decision, and it may take time for you to sort through all of your feelings about the relationship and its possible end. Therapy can help you have a space to be completely honest with yourself about your relationship as you grapple with your decision of whether to remain with your boyfriend. Your therapist can ask questions to guide you in uncovering your true feelings about whether this is the right relationship for you, and he or she can support you in whatever decision you come to.

instruction: When I got in trouble with the law for the first time, it was a shameful experience. I was more sad that I let me dad down.
output: I was ashamed when I got in trouble with the law. I was more ashamed of what my dad was going to think.

instruction: That phrase makes me crazy. It happens anytime I point out something to my husband that he needs to change, such as looking up from his iPad long enough for me to tell him the grandbaby almost pulled the shelf unit over or explaining to him that I got all the things he needs to bake a pie. Another example is when he opens the front door, the dog runs out if he doesn't pick him up. Over and over again, he lets the dog run out, and I am afraid he will get hit by a car.
output: It's hard to say what is okay and what is not okay, as it depends on what you and your husband feel is appropriate in your relationship. If you are feeling like you need to walk on eggshells or that you can't speak your mind and say how you feel, then that sounds like a problem. I would recommend having an open discussion with your husband about communication between the two of you in general. Do this during a time when there is little or no conflict and emotions are not heightened. Let him know how you feel and give him specific ideas of how you would like him to respond instead of saying, "enough." Additionally, try and see his perspective and understand why he shuts you down. Perhaps, he feels like he is being told what to do constantly or that he gets little positive feedback from you. An open dialogue about how to discuss issues going forward will likely help. Seeing a couple's therapist will also greatly assist with teaching better communication skills and seeing if there are underlying issues that need resolution. Best of luck to you guys.

instruction: No, i have talked to my advisor once and she is not that helpful as she is a professor herself. She told me its okay if I dont have an A, I am still passing. I just worked hard all semester, just to end with a 89.9.
output: I had a similar situation last semester where I held an A in my class all semester long until the end when it dropped to a B+ so I comletely understand the frustration here.

instruction: In particular during family gatherings (such as funerals), where there are different customs.
output: I think the best thing you can do is be respectful of each family member's wishes. Although everyone may not agree with one another, the most important thing you can do for each other is to respect their faith and beliefs instead of trying to sway them in a certain direction. There are many families that are united despite their differences. For example, a mother may be Catholic and the father Jewish. By introducing the children to each faith until they are ready to decide for themselves can be beneficial. Also, it's not uncommon for views to change as one ages or faces different life experiences. So don't get too discouraged if your child wants to explore different avenues. Creating a loving and accepting environment is most essential.

instruction: My mother takes care of niece whom my sister abandoned. She calls me every day complaining, but I don't want to hear it anymore.
output: This sounds stressful.You mentioned that you want to learn how to avoid them. I'm not sure whether you want to avoid them overall or if you would like to ask your mom to maybe only talk to you about it for a few minutes about your niece in each conversation that you have.Maybe you can connect your mom with some support. It sounds as if she has lost part of herself and/or is very stressed out and in talking with you about it because she trusts you enough for you to be someone she can speak openly, you are becoming stressed because of things that you cannot directly change. This is the presumptive based on the amount of information that you posted, but it sounds like there is a bit of a circle of stress here.I also wonder who you could talk with when you are stressed.It may be helpful to see a local mental health professional to help redefine some boundaries so you can have the type of relationship with your mother that you want without necessarily feeling stressed out by it every day.

instruction: I have a pen that reminds me of my grandfather.
output: I got a pen from my grandfather that reminds me of him whenever I use it.

instruction: I'm fine when we start becoming intimate, but out of nowhere, I will get a flashback of what happened to me in the past. I start hysterically crying and freaking out when my boyfriend obviously has done nothing to hurt me.
